data.title: Associated Banc-Corp targets 18%-20% 2026 loan growth while planning share repurchases in H2
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Associated Banc-Corp (ASB) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "Midway through 2026, delivering sustainable, profitable organic growth continues to be the primary focus for our company" (President, CEO & Director Andrew Harmening), adding, "we've already hit that target as of June 30" for the "9% to 10% organic C&I loan growth" goal, citing "the addition of over $600 million in balances during the second quarter."
  • "Our next major milestone is systems and branch conversion, which we expect to take place in October of this year" (President, CEO & Director Harmening), and he said the American National integration has progressed with cost saves identified and "it's becoming clear that we are positioned to drive organic growth momentum over time."
  • "We reported GAAP EPS of $0.63 in Q2 or $0.73 after adjusting for $24 million of nonrecurring cost" (President, CEO & Director Harmening), alongside acquisition-driven balance-sheet expansion, including: "total loans grew by 15% versus the prior quarter" and "total deposits and core customer deposits, both grew by 12% in Q2" after adding American National balances.
  • "Second quarter net interest income of $370 million, increased $63 million versus the prior quarter" (Executive VP & CFO Derek Meyer), and "our net interest margin increased 14 basis points to 3.17% for the quarter," while noting that purchase accounting and deferred loan cost/fee adjustments "combined to drive a 6 basis point improvement in our Q2 margin."
  • "To confirm, we are not finding any surprises in the American National portfolio relative to due diligence" (Executive VP, Chief Credit Officer & Chicago Market President Patrick Ahern), adding, "we remain confident in our integration, finding the portfolio modestly exceeding our expectations."

Outlook

  • "For the combined company, we now expect period-end total loan growth of 18% to 20% in 2026" (President, CEO & Director Harmening) and "continue to expect C&I loan growth of 20% to 22%, as compared to associated stand-alone results for the year ended December 31, 2025."
  • "We continue to expect 2026 period end total deposit growth of 17% to 19% and period-end core customer deposit growth of 19% to 21%" (President, CEO & Director Harmening), again framed "as compared to associated stand-alone results for the year ended December 31, 2025."
  • "We now expect a total 2026 net interest income to grow by 19% to 21%" (Executive VP & CFO Meyer), and "in 2026, we continue to expect total noninterest income growth of 8% to 10%."
  • "We now expect noninterest expense to grow by 20% to 21% in 2026" (Executive VP & CFO Meyer).
  • Compared with Q1, management raised combined-company loan growth to "18% to 20%" from "17% to 19%" (President, CEO & Director Harmening), while keeping deposit growth ranges at "17% to 19%" total and "19% to 21%" core customer (President, CEO & Director Harmening).

Financial Results

  • "Total noninterest income of $80 million increased by $5 million versus the prior quarter" (President, CEO & Director Harmening), and "total noninterest expense of $272 million increased by $53 million versus the prior quarter" with "$24 million onetime expenses tied to the deal."
  • "Within that transaction, we sold their securities portfolio with a book value of approximately $1 billion and reinvested the same amount at a yield of approximately 4.6%" (Executive VP & CFO Meyer).
  • "Our CET1 ratio finished at 10.47% in Q2" (Executive VP & CFO Meyer) and "our tangible book value per share finished at $22.15" (Executive VP & CFO Meyer).
  • "In Q2, our ACLL increased by $69 million to $494 million" (Executive VP, Chief Credit Officer & Chicago Market President Ahern) and "our ACL ratio as a percentage of total loans increased to 1.36%" (Chief Credit Officer Ahern).

Q&A

  • Brandon Rud, Stephens: asked to unpack the expense guide and a core 2026 number; Executive VP & CFO Meyer: "I would take the guide and just take out the numbers on Slide 5" and said core ASB was "pretty much on track with our original 3% guidance for core ASB."
  • Brandon Rud, Stephens: asked why total loan growth guidance rose while C&I was unchanged; President, CEO & Director Harmening: "It's largely coming from CRE" and said the second-half slowdown reflects "the timing of some production" and expected "pay downs" and "payouts" dynamics.
  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: pressed on quarterly expense run-rate and Q4 vs. Q3; Executive VP & CFO Meyer: "we're not ready to parse the quarters yet" and "it's going to be hard to pin me down to a quarterly run rate yet."
  • Casey Haire, Autonomous: asked about deposit costs and new-money loan yields; Executive VP & CFO Meyer: "our deposit pricing was sort of the unsung hero of the quarter" and said "deposit costs actually improved modestly second quarter at legacy ASB."
  • Jonathan Rau, Barclays: asked about reducing brokered/FHLB funding; Executive VP & CFO Meyer: "we expect even by the end of this year, for that as a mix, as a percent to be down a 1% to 2% versus what previously forecasted."
  • Jon Arfstrom, RBC: asked about buybacks; President, CEO & Director Harmening: "we're in a pretty good position to deploy the already approved share repurchases in the third quarter and the fourth quarter."
  • Christopher O'Connell, KBW: asked about nonaccrual and reserve trends post-acquisition; Executive VP, Chief Credit Officer & Chicago Market President Ahern: "about half of it came from the American National portfolio" and "I think we're going to be pretty steady" on the reserve ratio.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly skeptical on expense trajectory and run-rate, with repeated follow-ups on quarterly shaping; management resisted precision, with "we're not ready to parse the quarters yet" (Executive VP & CFO Meyer).
  • Management tone was slightly positive on profitability and funding, emphasizing momentum and capital actions, including "we believe we'll have NIM expansion in the third quarter" and "in the fourth quarter" (President, CEO & Director Harmening).
  • Versus Q1, Q2 included more definitive post-close statements on marks, cost saves, and capital actions (including buybacks), while Q1 language leaned more toward pending marks and "expected" updates.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q2 incorporated American National balances and finalized purchase accounting marks; Q1 repeatedly referenced that the company would "finalize purchase accounting adjustments" in Q2 (Executive VP & CFO Meyer).
  • Guidance language tightened post-close: Q2 set combined-company 2026 net interest income growth at "19% to 21%" (Executive VP & CFO Meyer) after Q1 indicated an update would follow marks.
  • Analyst focus shifted from pre-mark uncertainty in Q1 (margin/accretion and marks timing) to Q2 emphasis on expense normalization, cost saves timing, and capital deployment (including buybacks).

Risks and Concerns

  • "We continue to expect elevated payoffs in the back half of the year" (President, CEO & Director Harmening), and management described payoff timing uncertainty while setting guidance.
  • "Our teams remain vigilant" (Executive VP, Chief Credit Officer & Chicago Market President Ahern) amid "ongoing inflation pressures, shifting labor markets, tariffs and other economic concerns."
  • Integration execution risk centered on the October conversion milestone: "Our next major milestone is systems and branch conversion" (President, CEO & Director Harmening).

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that Q2 combined strong organic commercial momentum with the closed American National acquisition, highlighted by an expanded balance sheet, a 3.17% net interest margin, updated 2026 growth expectations (including 18% to 20% total loan growth), and increased cost-save expectations to about 30% of American National’s expense base. Executives repeatedly framed the October systems conversion as the next key integration milestone and said they expect to begin deploying already-approved share repurchases in the third and fourth quarters.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: These ETFs stand to benefit from Intel's post-earnings rally
data.description:
Intel (INTC) surged over 6% in premarket trading on Friday after the chipmaker delivered a third-quarter revenue forecast well above Wall Street expectations, bringing into the spotlight the exchange-traded funds with significant exposure to the stock.

The company projected third-quarter revenue of $15.8B to $16.8B, comfortably above the $15.1B consensus estimate. Intel also reported adjusted second-quarter earnings of $0.42 per share on revenue of $16.13B, up 25% from a year earlier. Its Data Center and AI business generated $6.26B in revenue, a 59% year-over-year increase that topped analysts' expectations of $5.54B, underscoring strong demand for AI infrastructure.

Intel's stronger-than-expected outlook adds to growing optimism that AI-driven spending on servers and data centers is accelerating the company's turnaround, making semiconductor- and technology-focused ETFs with sizable Intel positions potential beneficiaries if the stock extends its gains.

There are 614 ETFs with exposure to Intel (INTC).     

  • Direxion Daily INTC Bull 2X ETF (LINT) — 14.91% weighting
  • First Trust Nasdaq Semiconductor ETF (FTXL) — 11.47%
  • REX FANG & Innovation Equity Premium Income ETF (FEPI) — 6.47%
  • SPDR NYSE Technology ETF (XNTK) — 6.14%
  • Kurv Technology Titans Select ETF (KQQQ) — 6.12%
  • ProShares Nasdaq-100 Dorsey Wright Momentum ETF (QQQA) — 4.60%
  • First Trust S-Network Future Vehicles & Technology ETF (CARZ) — 4.23%
  • Adaptive Select ETF (ADPV) — 5.73%
  • Unusual Whales Subversive Republican Trading ETF (GOP) — 5.51%
  • iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) — 5.37%

Other ETFs with exposure to Intel include (AIBU), (DRIV), (CHPS), (BELT), (ESN), (XAIX), (FMCE), (CHPY), (EGGS), and (TTEQ).

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: INTC, SAP, and more
data.description:
Stock futures inched higher Friday morning as market participants weighed escalating military conflict in the Middle East against a busy week of technology sector earnings. 

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Safety Insurance (SAFT) +42% - Shares soared after the insurer agreed to be acquired by Mapfre in an all-cash deal valued at approximately $1.54B. Under the agreement, shareholders will receive $105 per share in cash, representing a 44% premium to the prior closing price. The transaction is expected to close in Q1 2027, subject to regulatory approvals, with Safety continuing to operate under its existing brand as a wholly owned subsidiary of Mapfre U.S.A.
  • Amkor Technology (AMKR) +9% - Shares jumped after the company announced a multi-year strategic partnership with Nvidia ((NVDA)) to develop advanced semiconductor packaging and testing technologies for next-generation AI platforms. As part of the deal, Nvidia will pay Amkor in advance to help increase its advanced packaging capacity in Arizona, which will boost U.S. AI chip production and make the supply chain more reliable, while also speeding up Amkor's growth and expansion in the U.S.
  • SAP SE (SAP) +6% - Shares gained after the enterprise software company beat Q2 EPS estimates, reported 24% growth in cloud revenue, and issued stronger-than-expected full-year guidance. Cloud backlog rose 26% Y/Y, topping expectations, while SAP raised its 2026 cloud and software revenue outlook above consensus. Management attributed the momentum to strong adoption of its Autonomous Enterprise strategy and growing demand for its Business AI Platform.
  • Intel (INTC) +3% - Shares climbed after the chipmaker beat Q2 revenue, EPS, and gross margin expectations and issued stronger-than-expected Q3 guidance. Revenue surged 25% to $16.1B, driven by robust Client Computing and Data Center & AI demand, while the adjusted gross margin of 41.8% also topped estimates. Intel forecast Q3 revenue of $15.8B-$16.8B and adjusted EPS of $0.38, both above consensus, as management highlighted accelerating AI-driven demand across its CPU, foundry, and advanced packaging businesses.

Biggest stock losers

  • MaxLinear (MXL) -10% - Shares plunged despite the semiconductor company beating Q2 revenue and earnings estimates and issuing a Q3 outlook well above consensus. Q2 revenue increased by 55% Y/Y to $168.8M, fueled by a remarkable 145% growth in AI data center infrastructure revenue. Management emphasized strong demand for its Keystone PAM4 DSP platform and an expanding optical networking pipeline. The company projected Q3 revenue in the range of $210M to $220M, with the midpoint of $215M well above the $173.8M consensus.
  • Deckers Outdoor (DECK) -3% - Shares declined despite the footwear maker surpassing Q2 EPS estimates, as its raised full-year profit outlook was still below consensus. The company posted its first-ever $1B quarterly revenue, driven by continued strength at HOKA and UGG, with gross margin expanding to 56.4%, well above expectations.

data.title: Intel rises after Q2 beat, but SA analysts highlight low expectations, Foundry business
data.description:
Shares of Intel (INTC) rose about 5% premarket on Friday after second-quarter results and guidance beat estimates. However, Seeking Alpha analysts highlighted low expectations making the "print look like a home run," and the Foundry segment, which "is still not really a well-working business."

"Back in early June, I downgraded Intel (INTC) to a sell following Nvidia (NVDA) penetrating the consumer PC processor market with the RTX Spark superchip. Part of what justified my sell at the time was that the stock was overvalued. What I’ve seen since then is the stock reset, dropping about 26% over the past month alone," said Seeking Alpha analyst The Techie. "I think this helped create a setup with low enough expectations that is making this print look like a home run. The results are good, but I’ve seen better results get punished this quarter. I think the market was looking for a positive to price in here, as the guide is better than expected, though the company still printed a sequential decline and soft margin expansion."

For the period ending June 27, Intel earned an adjusted $0.42 per share as revenue jumped 25% year-over-year to come in at $16.13B. The company expects third-quarter revenue to be between $15.8B and $16.8B, with the midpoint of $16.3B well above the $15.06B analysts were anticipating. 

"Intel has just posted a relatively strong quarter with revenue coming in at $16.1B, up 25% Y/Y and roughly above 12% of the consensus with non-GAAP EPS of 42 cents nearly doubling the consensus thanks to a surge of 59% in DCAI. More importantly Q3 guidance was relatively robust at a $16.3B midpoint," said Seeking Alpha analyst Louis Gerard.

However, the analyst noted that "the Foundry segment is still not really a well-working business as, yes it posted a revenue base of $5.77, which is up 31% Y/Y but it still posted an operating loss of -$2.09B and with the intersegment eliminations were set to -$5.48B so external foundry revenue only stood at a mere $300MM. And to be fair, the whole bull thesis behind INTC is about 18A external traction which has not been reflected in the earnings."

Gerard added that the $11B GAAP loss, which is not an impairment but an escrowed-shares derivative as the bridge is a $12.5B mark-to-market charge on shares held in escrow for the Commerce Department under the CHIPS Secure Enclave arrangement.

"Though it does not impact the overall operation it is worth noting that the higher INTC trades, the large the charge will be as the government’s claim appreciates with the equity," Gerard noted.
